title.zh: 卷四十七 文三王傳
title.en: Volume 47: The three sons of Emperor Wen
title.raw: 卷四十七 文三王傳 Volume 47: The three sons of Emperor Wen

sentences.zh: 卷四十七 文三王傳第十七

translations.literal: Scroll 47: Biography of the Three Kings of Wen, Number 17.
translations.idiomatic: Book 47: “The Three Princes of Emperor Wen,” Treatise 17.

sentences.zh: 孝文皇帝四男：竇皇后生孝景帝、梁孝王武，諸姬生代孝王參、梁懷王揖。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen had four sons: Empress Dou bore Emperor Xiaojing and Filial-and-Filial King Wu of Liang; various concubines bore Filial King Can of Dai and King Huai Yi of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen had four sons. Empress Dou gave birth to Emperor Jing and King Xiao Wu of Liang; other consorts bore King Xiao Can of Dai and King Huai Yi of Liang.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王武以孝文二年與太原王參、梁王揖同日立。

translations.literal: Filial-and-Filial King Wu of Liang, on the second year of Xiaowen, was established the same day as King Can of Taiyuan and King Yi of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of Emperor Wen’s reign, King Xiao Wu of Liang was enfeoffed on the same day as the King of Taiyuan, Can, and the King of Liang, Yi.

sentences.zh: 武為代王，四年徙為淮陽王，十二年徙梁，自初王通歷已十一年矣。

translations.literal: Wu was King of Dai; in the fourth year he was transferred to be King of Huaiyang; in the twelfth year he was transferred to Liang; from his first enfeoffment as king through the whole span it was already eleven years.
translations.idiomatic: He was first King of Dai, then in his fourth year as king was moved to Huaiyang, and in his twelfth year to Liang—eleven years in all from his initial appointment as king.

sentences.zh: 孝王十四年，入朝。

translations.literal: In the Filial King’s fourteenth year, he entered court for an audience.
translations.idiomatic: In his fourteenth year as king, he came to the capital for an audience.

sentences.zh: 十七年、十八年，比年入朝，留。

translations.literal: In the seventeenth and eighteenth years, in successive years he entered court and stayed.
translations.idiomatic: In the seventeenth and eighteenth years he came to court two years running and remained at the capital.

sentences.zh: 其明年，乃之國。

translations.literal: The next year, then he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he returned to his kingdom.

sentences.zh: 二十一年，入朝。

translations.literal: In the twenty-first year, he entered court.
translations.idiomatic: In his twenty-first year he came to court again.

sentences.zh: 二十二年，文帝崩。

translations.literal: In the twenty-second year, Emperor Wen died.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-second year Emperor Wen died.

sentences.zh: 二十四年，入朝。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fourth year, he entered court.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fourth year he came to court.

sentences.zh: 二十五年，復入朝。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fifth year, again he entered court.
translations.idiomatic: In the twenty-fifth year he came once more.

sentences.zh: 是時，上未置太子，與孝王宴飲，從容言曰：「千秋萬歲後傳於王。」

translations.literal: At this time the sovereign had not yet installed a crown prince; feasting and drinking with the Filial King, he spoke at ease, saying: “After a thousand autumns and ten thousand years I will transmit to the king.”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had not yet named an heir. At a banquet with the king of Liang he said, almost casually, that when he was gone he would pass the throne to him.

sentences.zh: 王辭謝。

translations.literal: The king declined and thanked him.
translations.idiomatic: The king demurred and thanked him.

sentences.zh: 雖知非至言，然心內喜。

translations.literal: Although he knew it was not ultimate truth, yet inwardly he was pleased.
translations.idiomatic: He knew it was not a binding pledge, yet he could not help rejoicing inwardly.

sentences.zh: 太后亦然。

translations.literal: The empress dowager was likewise so.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager felt the same way.

sentences.zh: 其春，吳、楚、齊、趙七國反，先擊梁棘壁，殺數萬人。

translations.literal: That spring, Wu, Chu, Qi, Zhao—the seven states rebelled; they first attacked Liang at Ji Rampart and killed several tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: That spring the seven kingdoms of Wu, Chu, Qi, Zhao, and the rest rose in revolt. Their first blow fell on Liang at Ji Rampart, where they slaughtered tens of thousands.

sentences.zh: 梁王城守睢陽，而使韓安國、張羽等為將軍以距吳、楚。

translations.literal: The King of Liang defended the city at Suiyang, and sent Han Anguo, Zhang Yu, and others as generals to resist Wu and Chu.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Liang held Suiyang while Han Anguo, Zhang Yu, and others led his troops against Wu and Chu.

sentences.zh: 吳、楚以梁為限，不敢過而西，與太尉亞夫等相距三月。

translations.literal: Wu and Chu took Liang as their limit; they did not dare pass west of it, and with Grand Commandant Zhou Yafu and others they faced each other for three months.
translations.idiomatic: Wu and Chu could not get past Liang to march west; for three months they were locked in stalemate with Grand Commandant Zhou Yafu and the imperial army.

sentences.zh: 吳、楚破，而梁所殺虜略與漢中分。

translations.literal: Wu and Chu were broken, and as for those whom Liang killed and captured, it was roughly half with Han.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu and Chu were crushed, the casualties Liang inflicted were roughly on a par with those of the central army.

sentences.zh: 明年，漢立太子。

translations.literal: The next year, Han established a crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the court formally invested a crown prince.

sentences.zh: 梁最親，有功，又為大國，居天下膏腴地，北界泰山，西至高陽，四十餘城，多大縣。

translations.literal: Liang was most closely related, had merit, and was again a great state; it occupied the fat and fertile land under heaven; its north border was Mount Tai, its west reached to Gaoyang; more than forty cities, many of them large counties.
translations.idiomatic: Liang was the emperor’s closest kin, had earned distinction in the war, and remained a vast domain—the richest soil in the realm, stretching from Mount Tai in the north to Gaoyang in the west, with over forty counties, many of them populous.

sentences.zh: 孝王，太后少子，愛之，賞賜不可勝道。

translations.literal: The Filial King was the empress dowager’s youngest son; she loved him; rewards and gifts could not be reckoned.
translations.idiomatic: As the empress dowager’s youngest son, King Xiao was her favorite; the gifts showered on him were beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 於是孝王築東苑，方三百餘里，廣睢陽城七十里，大治宮室，為復道，自宮連屬於平台三十餘里。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Filial King built the Eastern Park, more than three hundred li square; he widened the city of Suiyang seventy li; he greatly built palaces and halls, and made covered passages; from the palace continuous connection to the terrace platform was more than thirty li.
translations.idiomatic: He built the Eastern Park, over three hundred li on a side, expanded Suiyang’s walls to seventy li, raised palaces on a grand scale, and ran covered walkways more than thirty li from his residence to the terrace park.

sentences.zh: 得賜天子旌旗，從千乘萬騎，出稱警，入言蹕，擬於天子。

translations.literal: He obtained the gift of the Son of Heaven’s banners and flags; with a thousand chariots and ten thousand riders he went out declaring “jing” and entered saying “bi”; he was comparable to the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: He was allowed the imperial pennants and escort of a thousand chariots and ten thousand horsemen; when he left his precincts heralds cleared the way, and when he entered they enforced the halt—privileges that mirrored the emperor’s own.

sentences.zh: 招延四方豪桀，自山東游士莫不至：齊人羊勝、公孫詭、鄒陽之屬。

translations.literal: He summoned and retained bold men from the four quarters; from east of the mountains wandering scholars none failed to come: men of Qi such as Yang Sheng, Gongsun Gui, Zou Yang, and their sort.
translations.idiomatic: He drew in adventurers from every quarter; no notable wandering scholar east of the mountains failed to answer his call—among them Yang Sheng, Gongsun Gui, and Zou Yang of Qi.

sentences.zh: 公孫詭多奇邪計，初見日，王賜千金，官至中尉，號曰公孫將軍。

translations.literal: Gongsun Gui had many strange and wicked schemes; on the day he was first seen, the king gifted him a thousand in gold; his office reached to commandant of the capital guard; he was styled General Gongsun.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Gui trafficked in devious stratagems. The king gave him a thousand pieces of gold the day they met, raised him to commandant of the capital, and called him “General Gongsun.”

sentences.zh: 多作兵弩弓數十萬，而府庫金錢且百巨萬，珠玉寶器多於京師。

translations.literal: He made many tens of thousands of crossbows and bows, and the treasury’s cash and money approached a hundred “great myriads”; pearls, jade, and precious vessels exceeded those in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He stockpiled tens of thousands of crossbows and bows; his treasuries held nearly a hundred million in cash, and his hoard of jewels and curios outshone the imperial storehouses.

sentences.zh: 二十九年十月，孝王入朝。

translations.literal: In the twenty-ninth year, tenth month, the Filial King entered court.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth month of his twenty-ninth year as king he came to court.

sentences.zh: 景帝使使持乘輿駟，迎梁王於關下。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing sent envoys bearing the imperial four-horse carriage to welcome the King of Liang below the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing dispatched officials with the imperial team of four bays to meet the king of Liang outside the frontier passes.

sentences.zh: 既朝，上疏，因留。

translations.literal: Having had audience, he submitted a memorial and therefore stayed.
translations.idiomatic: After his audience he petitioned to remain, and was allowed to stay.

sentences.zh: 以太后故，入則侍帝同輦，出則同車遊獵上林中。

translations.literal: Because of the empress dowager, when entering he attended the emperor sharing the same palanquin; when going out he rode the same chariot hunting in Shanglin Park.
translations.idiomatic: For his mother’s sake he rode with the emperor in the same palanquin when they went in, and hunted together in the imperial park in the same chariot when they went out.

sentences.zh: 梁之侍中、郎、謁者著引籍出入天子殿門，與漢宦官亡異。

translations.literal: Liang’s palace attendants, gentlemen, and petitioners, wearing their tallies, passed in and out of the Son of Heaven’s hall gates; there was no difference from Han eunuchs.
translations.idiomatic: Liang’s household chamberlains, gentlemen-in-attendance, and ushers entered the palace gates on registry tallies exactly like the emperor’s own staff.

sentences.zh: 十一月，上廢栗太子，太后心欲以梁王為嗣。

translations.literal: In the eleventh month, the sovereign deposed the Crown Prince of Su; the empress dowager in her heart wished to make the King of Liang heir.
translations.idiomatic: That November the emperor deposed the heir from the Su clan; the empress dowager began to hope openly that the king of Liang might succeed him.

sentences.zh: 大臣及爰盎等有所關說於帝，太后議格，孝王不敢復言太后以嗣事。

translations.literal: Great ministers and Yuan Ang and others had explanations they presented to the emperor; the empress dowager’s deliberations were blocked; the Filial King did not dare again speak to the empress dowager about the heir matter.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers led by Yuan Ang remonstrated with the emperor until the dowager’s plan stalled; the king of Liang never again raised the succession with his mother.

sentences.zh: 事秘，世莫知，乃辭歸國。

translations.literal: The affair was secret; the age did not know; then he took leave and returned to his state.
translations.idiomatic: The matter was kept so close that the world knew nothing of it until the king asked leave and went home.

sentences.zh: 其夏，上立膠東王為太子。

translations.literal: That summer, the sovereign established the King of Jiaodong as crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: That summer the emperor named the prince of Jiaodong crown prince.

sentences.zh: 梁王怨爰盎及議臣，乃與羊勝、公孫詭之屬謀，陰使人刺殺爰盎及他議臣十餘人。

translations.literal: The King of Liang resented Yuan Ang and the deliberating ministers; then with Yang Sheng, Gongsun Gui, and their sort he plotted; secretly he sent men to assassinate Yuan Ang and more than ten other deliberating ministers.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Liang blamed Yuan Ang and the ministers who had opposed him. With Yang Sheng, Gongsun Gui, and their confederates he arranged the secret murder of Yuan Ang and more than a dozen others who had spoken against him.

sentences.zh: 賊未得也。

translations.literal: The bandits were not yet obtained.
translations.idiomatic: The assassins were not caught.

sentences.zh: 於是天子意梁，逐賊，果梁使之。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven’s mind was on Liang; pursuing the bandits, it turned out Liang had sent them.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor’s suspicions fell on Liang; when the trail was followed, the killers proved to have been sent from Liang.

sentences.zh: 遣使冠蓋相望於道，復案梁事。

translations.literal: He dispatched envoys; canopies and covers filled the road one after another; again he investigated Liang’s affair.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial messengers shuttled along the roads while the court reopened the case against Liang.

sentences.zh: 捕公孫詭、羊勝，皆匿王后宮。

translations.literal: They arrested Gongsun Gui and Yang Sheng; both hid in the queen’s palace.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Gui and Yang Sheng were ordered seized, but both had taken refuge in the queen’s apartments.

sentences.zh: 使者責二千石急，梁相軒丘豹及內史安國皆泣諫王，王乃令勝、詭皆自殺，出之。

translations.literal: The envoys pressed the two-thousand-bushel officials urgently; Liang’s chancellor Xuanqiu Bao and inner scribe Anguo all wept and remonstrated with the king; the king then ordered Sheng and Gui both to kill themselves and produced them.
translations.idiomatic: Under relentless pressure from the envoys, Chancellor Xuanqiu Bao and Inner Scribe Anguo wept as they urged the king to yield; at last he had Yang Sheng and Gongsun Gui kill themselves and handed over their bodies.

sentences.zh: 上由此怨望於梁王。

translations.literal: The sovereign from this harbored resentment toward the King of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: From that day the emperor nursed a grudge against the king of Liang.

sentences.zh: 梁王恐，乃使韓安國因長公主謝罪太后，然後得釋。

translations.literal: The King of Liang was afraid; then he sent Han Anguo through the Eldest Princess to beg forgiveness of the empress dowager; afterward he obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: Terrified, he sent Han Anguo to plead through the Eldest Princess before the empress dowager, and only then escaped worse punishment.

sentences.zh: 上怒稍解，因上書請朝。

translations.literal: The sovereign’s anger gradually eased; therefore he submitted a memorial requesting audience.
translations.idiomatic: As the emperor’s wrath cooled, the king petitioned for permission to come to court.

sentences.zh: 既至關，茅蘭說王，使乘布車，從兩騎入，匿於長公主園。

translations.literal: Having reached the passes, Mao Lan persuaded the king to ride a cloth-covered cart with two riders following, and hide in the Eldest Princess’s garden.
translations.idiomatic: When he reached the frontier, Mao Lan advised him to enter in a plain covered cart with only two outriders and conceal himself in the Eldest Princess’s villa.

sentences.zh: 漢使迎王，王已入關，車騎盡居外，外不知王處。

translations.literal: Han’s envoys welcomed the king; the king had already entered the passes; chariots and riders all remained outside; those outside did not know where the king was.
translations.idiomatic: The imperial escort came to meet him, but he had already slipped through the gates; his train waited outside while no one knew his whereabouts.

sentences.zh: 太后泣曰：「帝殺吾子！」

translations.literal: The empress dowager wept, saying: “The emperor has killed my son!”
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager wept, “The emperor has murdered my son!”

sentences.zh: 帝憂恐。

translations.literal: The emperor was anxious and afraid.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was stricken with fear.

sentences.zh: 於是梁王伏斧質，之闕下謝罪。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Liang lay on the axe and block and went below the palace gate to beg forgiveness.
translations.idiomatic: Then the king of Liang appeared at the palace gate with the executioner’s axe and chopping-block across his neck to offer his submission.

sentences.zh: 然後太后、帝皆大喜，相與泣，復如故。

translations.literal: Afterward the empress dowager and the emperor were both greatly pleased; they wept together with each other and returned as before.
translations.idiomatic: Mother and son embraced in tears and were reconciled as before.

sentences.zh: 悉召王從官入關。

translations.literal: They summoned all the king’s followers to enter the passes.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered every member of the king’s entourage admitted through the passes.

sentences.zh: 然帝益疏王，不與同車輦矣。

translations.literal: Yet the emperor increasingly kept the king at a distance and did not share the same palanquin with him.
translations.idiomatic: Even so, the emperor grew cooler toward him and never again shared a palanquin.

sentences.zh: 三十五年冬，復入朝。

translations.literal: In the thirty-fifth year, winter, again he entered court.
translations.idiomatic: In the winter of his thirty-fifth year as king he returned to the capital.

sentences.zh: 上疏欲留，上弗許。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial wishing to stay; the sovereign did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: He asked leave to remain; the emperor refused.

sentences.zh: 歸國，意忽忽不樂。

translations.literal: Returning to his state, his mind was absent and unhappy.
translations.idiomatic: Back in his kingdom he sank into restless gloom.

sentences.zh: 北獵梁山，有獻牛，足上出背上，孝王惡之。

translations.literal: Hunting north at Mount Liang, someone presented an ox whose feet emerged on its back; the Filial King detested it.
translations.idiomatic: While hunting north of Mount Liang he was given an ox whose hooves grew from its back—a monstrous omen he found deeply ominous.

sentences.zh: 六月中，病熱，六日薨。

translations.literal: In the sixth month, he was ill with fever; on the sixth day he died.
translations.idiomatic: He took fever in the sixth month and died on the sixth day.

sentences.zh: 孝王慈孝，每聞太后病，口不能食，常欲留長安侍太后。

translations.literal: The Filial King was kind and filial; every time he heard the empress dowager was ill, his mouth could not eat; he always wished to stay in Chang’an to attend the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: The king was devoted to his mother: whenever he heard she was unwell he lost his appetite and longed to stay in Chang’an to nurse her.

sentences.zh: 太后亦愛之。

translations.literal: The empress dowager also loved him.
translations.idiomatic: She loved him in return.

sentences.zh: 及聞孝王死，竇太后泣極哀，不食，曰：「帝果殺吾子！」

translations.literal: When she heard the Filial King had died, Empress Dou wept with extreme grief, did not eat, saying: “The emperor truly killed my son!”
translations.idiomatic: When news came that King Xiao had died, Empress Dou wept until she could not eat, crying that the emperor had truly killed her son.

sentences.zh: 帝哀懼，不知所為。

translations.literal: The emperor was sorrowful and afraid and did not know what to do.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was grief-stricken and at a loss.

sentences.zh: 與長公主計之，乃分梁為五國，盡立孝王男五人為王，女五人皆令食湯沐邑。

translations.literal: He consulted with the Eldest Princess; then they divided Liang into five states, all establishing the Filial King’s five sons as kings; the five daughters all were ordered to receive bathing-fief towns.
translations.idiomatic: He and the Eldest Princess agreed to split Liang into five kingdoms, enfeoffing each of the king’s five sons as a ruler and assigning each of his five daughters an income from a bathing-fief town.

sentences.zh: 奏之太后，太后乃說，為帝壹餐。

translations.literal: They presented it to the empress dowager; the empress dowager then was pleased and for the emperor ate one meal.
translations.idiomatic: When this was laid before the empress dowager she relented and took a single meal for her son’s sake.

sentences.zh: 孝王未死時，財以巨萬計，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Before the Filial King died, his wealth was reckoned in “great myriads” and could not be counted.
translations.idiomatic: Before his death his fortune already ran to uncountable millions.

sentences.zh: 及死，藏府余黃金尚四十餘萬斤，他財物稱是。

translations.literal: When he died, the storehouses still had more than forty myriads of jin of yellow gold; other wealth matched this.
translations.idiomatic: At his death the treasuries still held over forty ten-thousand jin of gold, with goods to match.

sentences.zh: 地節中，冀州刺史林奏年為太子時與女弟則私通。

translations.literal: In the Dijie era, the Inspector of Ji Province Lin memorialized that when Nian was crown prince he had illicit relations with his younger sister Ze.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan’s Dijie reign, Ji provincial inspector Lin reported that in his crown-prince days Liu Nian had carried on an incestuous affair with his younger sister Ze.

sentences.zh: 及年立為王后，則懷年子，其婿使勿舉。

translations.literal: When Nian was established as queen, Ze was pregnant with Nian’s child; her husband ordered that it not be brought to birth.
translations.idiomatic: After Nian became king of Dai, Ze conceived his child; her husband ordered the pregnancy concealed and the child not delivered.

sentences.zh: 則曰：「自來殺之。」

translations.literal: Ze said: “I myself will kill it.”
translations.idiomatic: Ze said she would do away with the infant herself.

sentences.zh: 婿怒曰：「為王生子，自令王家養之。」

translations.literal: The husband angrily said: “Bearing a son for the king, naturally order the king’s house to raise him.”
translations.idiomatic: Her husband retorted, “If you bear a prince for the royal house, let the royal house raise him.”

sentences.zh: 則送兒頃太后所。

translations.literal: Ze sent the child to Empress Dowager Qing’s residence.
translations.idiomatic: Ze sent the infant to the apartments of Empress Dowager Qing, the queen mother at Dai.

sentences.zh: 相聞知，禁止則，令不得入宮。

translations.literal: The chancellor heard and knew; he forbade Ze and ordered that she not be allowed to enter the palace.
translations.idiomatic: When the chancellor learned of it he barred Ze from the palace.

sentences.zh: 年使從季父往來送迎則，連年不絕。

translations.literal: Nian sent his paternal uncle’s son to go back and forth escorting Ze; year after year it did not stop.
translations.idiomatic: Nian kept sending a cousin to shuttle Ze in and out, year after year without pause.

sentences.zh: 有司奏年淫亂，年坐廢為庶人，徙房陵，與湯沐邑百戶。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized that Nian was debauched and disorderly; Nian sat guilty and was demoted to commoner, moved to Fangling, with a bathing fief of one hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities impeached Nian for sexual misconduct; he was stripped of rank, banished to Fangling, and left with a stipend of only a hundred households.

sentences.zh: 立三年，國除。

translations.literal: Established three years, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Three years after his enfeoffment the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 元始二年，新都侯王莽興滅繼絕，白太皇太后，立年弟子如意為廣宗王，奉代孝王后。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanshi, Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang raised the fallen and continued the cut off; he informed the Grand Empress Dowager and established Nian’s younger brother’s son Ruyi as King of Guangzong, to serve the Filial Queen of Dai.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshi 2 Wang Mang, as marquis of Xindu, petitioned the Grand Empress Dowager to revive a fallen line and enfeoffed Nian’s nephew Ruyi as king of Guangzong to continue the sacrifices to King Xiao of Dai’s queen.

sentences.zh: 莽篡位，國絕。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped the throne, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized the throne the line ended again.

sentences.zh: 梁懷王揖，文帝少子也。

translations.literal: King Huai Yi of Liang, he was Emperor Wen’s youngest son.
translations.idiomatic: King Huai Yi of Liang was the last son born to Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 好《詩》、《書》，帝愛之，異於他子。

translations.literal: He loved the Odes and Documents; the emperor loved him, different from other sons.
translations.idiomatic: He loved the Classics of Poetry and History, and the emperor favored him above his other sons.

sentences.zh: 五年一朝，凡再入朝。

translations.literal: Once every five years an audience; in all twice he entered court.
translations.idiomatic: He was required to attend court once every five years and did so twice in all.

sentences.zh: 因墮馬死，立十年薨。

translations.literal: Because of falling from a horse he died; established ten years he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died from a fall from his horse in the tenth year of his reign.

sentences.zh: 無子，國除。

translations.literal: He had no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He left no heir, so the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 明年，梁孝王武徙王梁。

translations.literal: The next year, Filial King Wu of Liang was transferred to be king of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: The following year King Xiao Wu of Liang was moved to the vacant Liang domain.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王子五人為王。

translations.literal: The Filial King’s five sons became kings.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao’s five sons were each made kings.

sentences.zh: 太子買為梁共王，次子明為濟川王，彭離為濟東王，定為山陽王，不識為濟陰王，皆以孝景中六年同日立。

translations.literal: The crown prince Mai was King Gong of Liang; the second son Ming was King of Jichuan; Pengli was King of Jidong; Ding was King of Shanyang; Bushi was King of Jiyin; all were established the same day in the sixth year of Xiaojing’s middle period.
translations.idiomatic: The heir Mai became King Gong of Liang; his brothers Ming, Pengli, Ding, and Bushi received Jichuan, Jidong, Shanyang, and Jiyin respectively—all invested on the same day in the sixth year of Emperor Jing’s mid-reign.

sentences.zh: 梁共王買立七年薨，子平王襄嗣。

translations.literal: King Gong of Liang Mai, established seven years, died; his son Ping King Xiang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Gong Mai ruled seven years and died; his son King Xiang the Peaceful succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 濟川王明以垣邑侯立。

translations.literal: King Ming of Jichuan was established from Marquis of Yuanyi.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Jichuan, Ming, had previously held the marquisate of Yuanyi.

sentences.zh: 七年，坐射殺其中尉，有司請誅，武帝弗忍，廢為庶人，徙房陵，國除。

translations.literal: In the seventh year, sitting guilty of shooting and killing his lieutenant in the capital, the responsible officials requested execution; Emperor Wu could not bear it; he was demoted to commoner, moved to Fangling, the state abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In his seventh year he was convicted of murdering his capital commandant with a crossbow; though officials demanded his death, Emperor Wu spared his life, stripped him of rank, banished him to Fangling, and abolished his kingdom.

sentences.zh: 濟東王彭離立二十九年。

translations.literal: King Pengli of Jidong was established twenty-nine years.
translations.idiomatic: King Pengli of Jidong reigned twenty-nine years.

sentences.zh: 彭離驕悍，昏暮私與其奴亡命少年數十人行剽，殺人取財物以為好。

translations.literal: Pengli was arrogant and fierce; at dusk in private with his slaves and dozens of young men who had fled their obligations he went plundering; killing people and taking their goods and wealth he took as his pleasure.
translations.idiomatic: Pengli was violent and headstrong. At nightfall he would slip out with slaves and a band of young outlaws to rob and kill for sport.

sentences.zh: 所殺發覺者百餘人，國皆知之，莫敢夜行。

translations.literal: Those killed and discovered were more than a hundred; the whole state knew it; none dared travel by night.
translations.idiomatic: More than a hundred victims were traced to him; everyone in the kingdom knew, and no one dared walk abroad after dark.

sentences.zh: 所殺者子上書告言，有司請誅，武帝弗忍，廢為庶人，徙上庸，國除，為大河郡。

translations.literal: The son of one of those killed submitted a memorial accusing him; the responsible officials requested execution; Emperor Wu could not bear it; he was demoted to commoner, moved to Shangyong, the state abolished, becoming Great River commandery.
translations.idiomatic: When a victim’s son petitioned the throne, officials again demanded his life; the emperor commuted the sentence to commoner status, exile to Shangyong, and annexation of the territory as Great River commandery.

sentences.zh: 山陽哀王定立九年薨。

translations.literal: King Ding the Lamented of Shanyang was established nine years and died.
translations.idiomatic: King Ai Ding of Shanyang died in his ninth year.

sentences.zh: 亡子，國除。

translations.literal: He had no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He had no heir, so the kingdom was struck off.

sentences.zh: 濟陰哀王不識立一年薨。

translations.literal: King Bushi the Lamented of Jiyin was established one year and died.
translations.idiomatic: King Ai Bushi of Jiyin died after a single year on the throne.

sentences.zh: 亡子，國除。

translations.literal: He had no son; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He too left no son, and the fief lapsed.

sentences.zh: 孝王支子四王，皆絕於身。

translations.literal: The Filial King’s four sons by secondary wives, four kings, all ended in their own persons.
translations.idiomatic: The four collateral kings descended from King Xiao all died without issue.

sentences.zh: 梁平王襄，母曰陳太后。

translations.literal: Ping King Xiang of Liang; his mother was called Empress Dowager Chen.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiang the Peaceful of Liang was the son of Empress Dowager Chen.

sentences.zh: 共王母曰李太后。

translations.literal: King Gong’s mother was called Empress Dowager Li.
translations.idiomatic: King Gong’s mother was Empress Dowager Li.

sentences.zh: 李太后，親平王之大母也。

translations.literal: Empress Dowager Li was in person Ping King’s paternal grandmother (大母).
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Li was King Xiang’s grandmother on his father’s side—King Gong’s mother—by direct descent.

sentences.zh: 而平王之後曰任後，任後甚有寵於襄。

translations.literal: But Ping King’s queen was called Ren Hou; Ren Hou was greatly favored by Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiang’s chief consort was Lady Ren, and she held his affection completely.

sentences.zh: 元朔中，睢陽人犴反，人辱其父，而與睢陽太守客俱出同車。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshuo period, a man of Suiyang named Han Fan—someone had insulted his father—and he rode out in the same carriage with a client of the Suiyang grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu’s Yuanshuo reign, a Suiyang commoner named Han Fan, whose father had been publicly shamed, happened to share a carriage leaving town with one of the grand administrator’s retainers.

sentences.zh: 犴反殺其仇車上，亡去。

translations.literal: Han Fan killed his enemy on the carriage and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Fan stabbed his enemy to death in the cart and fled.

sentences.zh: 睢陽太守怒，以讓梁二千石。

translations.literal: The Suiyang grand administrator was angry and reproached Liang’s two-thousand-bushel officials.
translations.idiomatic: The grand administrator was furious and called Liang’s senior officials to account.

sentences.zh: 二千石以下求反急，執反親戚。

translations.literal: Below the rank of two thousand bushels they urgently sought Fan and seized Fan’s kin.
translations.idiomatic: Officials down the line hunted Fan relentlessly and rounded up his relatives.

sentences.zh: 反知國陰事，乃上變告梁王與大母爭尊狀。

translations.literal: Fan knew the kingdom’s hidden affairs; then he submitted a denunciation reporting the King of Liang and the circumstance of contending for precedence with the dowager grandmother (大母).
translations.idiomatic: Fan, knowing the court’s dirty secrets, filed a capital denunciation accusing the king of Liang of lording it over his grandmother, Empress Dowager Li, in matters of precedence.

sentences.zh: 時相以下具知之，欲以傷梁長吏，書聞。

translations.literal: At the time from the chancellor downward all knew of it; they wished thereby to injure Liang’s chief officials; the memorial was forwarded.
translations.idiomatic: From the chancellor on down everyone already knew the story; they let the memorial go up the chain, hoping to wound Liang’s high commissioners.

sentences.zh: 天子下吏驗問，有之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven sent down officials to verify and question; there was substance to it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor ordered an inquiry, and the charges proved true.

sentences.zh: 公卿治，奏以為不孝，請誅王及太后。

translations.literal: The high ministers tried the case; they memorialized deeming it unfilial conduct and requested execution of the king and the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: The council of state tried the affair, ruled it grossly unfilial, and asked that both the king and the dowager empress be put to death.

sentences.zh: 天子曰：「首惡失道，任後也。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven said: “The principal evildoer who lost the way was Consort Ren.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor replied, “The real culprit who abandoned moral duty was Consort Ren.”

sentences.zh: 朕置相吏不逮，無以輔王，故陷不誼，不忍致法。」

translations.literal: I appointed a chancellor and officials who fell short; there was none to assist the king; therefore he sank into unrighteousness; I cannot bear to apply full punishment.”
translations.idiomatic: I chose tutors and ministers who were not up to the task and failed to guide him, which led him into wrongdoing—but I will not press the matter to the letter of the law.”

sentences.zh: 削梁王五縣，奪王太后湯沐成陽邑，梟任後首於市，中郎胡等皆伏誅。

translations.literal: They stripped five counties from the King of Liang, confiscated the king’s empress dowager’s bathing fief at Chengyang, displayed Consort Ren’s head in the market; Palace Gentleman Hu and others were all executed.
translations.idiomatic: Five counties were cut from Liang, the dowager’s bathing-town income at Chengyang was seized, Consort Ren’s head was spiked in the marketplace, and Palace Gentleman Hu and his accomplices went to the block.

sentences.zh: 梁余尚有八城。

translations.literal: Liang still had eight cities remaining.
translations.idiomatic: Eight cities were left to the kingdom of Liang.

sentences.zh: 襄立四十年薨，子頃王無傷嗣。

translations.literal: Xiang was established forty years and died; his son King Qing Wushang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiang ruled forty years and died; his son Wushang succeeded as King Qing of Liang.

sentences.zh: 十一年薨，子敬王定國嗣。

translations.literal: Eleven years, died; his son King Jing Dingguo succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Eleven years later King Qing died, and his son Dingguo succeeded as King Jing.

sentences.zh: 四十年薨，子夷王遂嗣。

translations.literal: Forty years, died; his son King Yi Sui succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Jing reigned forty years; his son Sui succeeded as King Yi.

sentences.zh: 六年薨，子荒王嘉嗣。

translations.literal: Six years, died; his son King Huang Jia succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: King Yi died in his sixth year; his son Jia succeeded as King Huang.

sentences.zh: 十五年薨，子立嗣。

translations.literal: Fifteen years, died; his son Li succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen years later King Huang died, and his son Li inherited the kingdom.

sentences.zh: 鴻嘉中，太傅輔奏：「立一日至十一犯法，臣下愁苦，莫敢親近，不可諫止。

translations.literal: In the Hongjia period, Grand Tutor Fu memorialized: “Li in one day up to eleven times broke the law; his subordinates were bitterly troubled; none dared draw near; he could not be remonstrated with or stopped.
translations.idiomatic: During Hongjia, Grand Tutor Fu reported to the throne that in a single day King Li had broken the law as many as eleven times, leaving his staff demoralized and unable either to approach him or to check his excesses.

sentences.zh: 願令王，非耕、祠，法駕毋得出宮，盡出馬置外苑，收兵杖藏私府，毋得以金錢財物假賜人。」

translations.literal: We beg to order the king: except for ploughing and sacrifice, he must not leave the palace with the statutory chariot; send out all horses to the outer park; collect weapons and staves and store them in the privy treasury; he must not lend money or goods to others.”
translations.idiomatic: Fu asked that the king be confined to his palace except when using the prescribed chariot for farming or ancestral rites, that his horses be quartered in the outer park, his arms locked in the privy treasury, and that he be forbidden to hand out cash or gifts.

sentences.zh: 事下丞相、御史，請許。

translations.literal: The affair was sent down to the chancellor and imperial clerk; they requested permission.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial was referred to the chancellor and the censorate, which recommended approval.

sentences.zh: 奏可。

translations.literal: The memorial was approved.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved the request.

sentences.zh: 後數復驅傷郎，夜私出宮。

translations.literal: Afterward several times again he drove and injured gentlemen of the palace; at night he privately left the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Soon he was racing his chariot again, injuring palace attendants, and slipping out of the palace after dark.

sentences.zh: 傅相連奏，坐削或千戶或五百戶，如是者數焉。

translations.literal: Tutor and chancellor jointly memorialized; he sat guilty and was stripped sometimes of a thousand households, sometimes of five hundred; thus it happened several times.
translations.idiomatic: His tutor and chancellor filed repeated complaints; each time he lost either five hundred or a thousand households of revenue from his fief.

sentences.zh: 荒王女弟園子為立舅任寶妻，寶兄子昭為立後。

translations.literal: King Huang’s younger sister, the lady Yuánzi, was wife to Li’s maternal uncle Ren Bao; Bao’s elder brother’s son Zhao was Li’s empress.
translations.idiomatic: King Huang’s younger sister Lady Yuánzi was married to Li’s uncle Ren Bao, and Bao’s nephew Ren Zhao had become Li’s queen.

sentences.zh: 數過寶飲食，報寶曰：「我好翁主，欲得之。」

translations.literal: Several times he passed Bao’s to drink and eat; he reported to Bao: “I fancy the princess imperial; I wish to obtain her.”
translations.idiomatic: Li began visiting Bao’s house for feasts and confided that he coveted the princess and meant to have her.

sentences.zh: 寶曰：「翁主，姑也，法重。」

translations.literal: Bao said: “The princess imperial is your aunt; the law is severe.”
translations.idiomatic: Bao warned him, “That princess is your aunt by marriage; the statutes treat such a union harshly.”

sentences.zh: 立曰：「何能為！」

translations.literal: Li said: “What can it do!”
translations.idiomatic: Li replied, “What is there to fear?”

sentences.zh: 遂與園子奸。

translations.literal: Thereupon he committed adultery with Yuanzi.
translations.idiomatic: He then seduced his aunt Yuanzi.

sentences.zh: 積數歲，永始中，相禹奏立對外家怨望，有惡言。

translations.literal: Accumulating several years, in the Yongshi period, Chancellor Yu memorialized that Li, toward his wife’s kin, bore resentment and had evil words.
translations.idiomatic: Years later, in the Yongshi era, Chancellor Yu impeached Li for muttering slander against his in-laws.

sentences.zh: 有司案驗，因發淫亂事，奏立禽獸行，請誅。

translations.literal: The responsible officials investigated and verified; thereby they uncovered the debauched affair; they memorialized that Li had the conduct of birds and beasts; they requested execution.
translations.idiomatic: A full investigation exposed the incest; the prosecutors denounced Li for bestial conduct and demanded his life.

sentences.zh: 太中大夫谷永上疏曰：「臣聞『禮，天子外屏，不欲見外』也。

translations.literal: Grand Counsellor of the Palace Gu Yong submitted a memorial, saying: “Your servant has heard, ‘In ritual, the Son of Heaven has an outer screen; he does not wish to look upon the outer.’
translations.idiomatic: Gu Yong of the Palace submitted a long memorial: “The Rites prescribe an outer screen for the Son of Heaven so that he need not gaze upon what lies beyond.”

sentences.zh: 是故帝王之意，不窺人閨門之私，聽聞中□之言。

translations.literal: Therefore the intention of emperors and kings is not to peep into the private affairs of others’ inner chambers, nor to listen to words from within the inner [text damaged].
translations.idiomatic: For that reason true kings do not pry into bedroom secrets or traffic in gossip from the inner quarters [one character illegible in the received text].

sentences.zh: 《春秋》為親者諱。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn conceals for kin.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn Annals teach us to cover the shame of those near the throne.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云『戚戚兄弟，莫遠具爾』。

translations.literal: The Odes says, ‘Anxious anxious are brothers; do not distance yourselves from one another.’
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes put it, ‘Brothers should cling to one another, not stand aloof.’

sentences.zh: 今梁王年少，頗有狂病，始以惡言按驗，既亡事實，而發閨門之私，非本章所指。

translations.literal: Now the King of Liang is young and somewhat deranged; at first because of evil words he was investigated; already there are no facts, yet the private affairs of the inner gate are exposed; this is not what the original chapter intended.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Liang is young and somewhat unstable; you began with vague charges of slander, found no solid proof, and are now airing bedroom scandal—far from the point of the original accusation.

sentences.zh: 王辭又不服，猥強劾立，傅致難明之事，獨以偏辭成罪斷獄，亡益於治道。

translations.literal: The king’s plea also does not submit; basely and forcibly impeaching Li, attaching hard-to-clarify matters, alone by partial words completing guilt and deciding the prison case—there is no benefit to the way of rule.
translations.idiomatic: He denies the indictment, yet you would convict him on strained charges and one-sided testimony, which does nothing for good government.

sentences.zh: 污蔑宗室，以內亂之惡披布宣揚於天下，非所以為公族隱諱，增朝廷之榮華，昭聖德之風化也。

translations.literal: Slandering the imperial house, spreading the evil of internal disorder over the whole world under heaven—this is not the means to conceal shame for the ducal lineage, increase the court’s glory, or display sagely virtue’s transforming influence.
translations.idiomatic: To drag the Liu house through the mire and broadcast a tale of incest across the realm neither shields the imperial clan nor adds luster to the throne or to sagely example.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為王少，而父同產長，年齒不倫；

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks the king is young, while his father’s uterine kin are elders; their years and standing do not match;
translations.idiomatic: The king is still a youth, while his father’s cousins by the same mother are grown men—there is no parity between them;

sentences.zh: 梁國之富，足以厚聘美女，招致妖麗；

translations.literal: Liang’s wealth is sufficient to richly engage beautiful women and summon bewitching loveliness;
translations.idiomatic: Liang is rich enough to buy any beauty he wants;

sentences.zh: 父同產亦有恥辱之心。

translations.literal: the father’s uterine kin also have a mind of shame.
translations.idiomatic: and his father’s cousins still retain some sense of shame.

sentences.zh: 案事者乃驗問惡言，何故猥自發舒？

translations.literal: Those who handle the case then verify and question evil words—why basely themselves disclose and expand?
translations.idiomatic: If investigators press only on loose talk, why should they themselves broadcast every lurid detail?

sentences.zh: 以三者揆之，殆非人情，疑有所迫切，過誤失言，文吏躡尋，不得轉移。

translations.literal: Measuring by these three, it is probably not human feeling; I suspect there was something pressing, a mistake in speech, and clerks followed the trail unable to shift course.
translations.idiomatic: Weigh those three points and the story strains credulity: I suspect someone spoke rashly under pressure, and petty clerks simply ran with it.

sentences.zh: 萌牙之時，加恩勿治，上也。

translations.literal: At the time of sprouting buds, add grace and do not prosecute—this is the highest course.
translations.idiomatic: The best policy is to show mercy while the offense is still a bud and let the matter drop.

sentences.zh: 既已案驗舉憲，宜及王辭不服，詔廷尉選上德通理之吏，更審考清問，著不然之效，定失誤之法，而反命於下吏，以廣公族附疏之德，為宗室刷污亂之恥，甚得治親之誼。」

translations.literal: Already having investigated and raised the statute, it is fitting, since the king’s plea does not submit, to order the commandant of justice to select a superior and principled official to examine anew with clear questioning, set forth the appearance of untruth, fix the law of error, and in turn charge the lower clerks—thereby extending grace to the collateral branches of the ducal lineage, for the imperial house brushing away the shame of defilement and disorder; this greatly obtains the righteousness of ruling kin.”
translations.idiomatic: If you must pursue it, then while the king still protests his innocence, name an upright judge to rehear the case, expose any fabrication, correct procedural abuse, and discipline the minor officials who stirred the pot—thus showing generosity to distant kin of the throne and wiping the stain from the house of Liu. That would be true justice among kinsmen.”

sentences.zh: 天子由是寢而不治。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven from this shelved it and did not prosecute.
translations.idiomatic: On that plea the emperor shelved the case and took no further action.

sentences.zh: 居數歲，元延中，立復以公事怨相掾及睢陽丞，使奴殺之，殺奴以滅口。

translations.literal: Dwelling several years, in the Yuanyan period, Li again because of public affairs resented the chancellor’s clerk and the Suiyang assistant magistrate; he sent slaves to kill them, and killed the slaves to silence mouths.
translations.idiomatic: Some years into Yuanyan, Li nursed a grudge against a secretary of his chancellor and the assistant magistrate of Suiyang over routine business; he had them murdered by slaves, then murdered the slaves to cover his tracks.

sentences.zh: 凡殺三人，傷五人，手驅郎吏二十餘人。

translations.literal: In all he killed three persons, injured five, and personally drove his chariot against more than twenty gentlemen and clerks.
translations.idiomatic: In all he took three lives, wounded five men, and personally drove down more than twenty palace attendants and officials.

sentences.zh: 上書不拜奏。

translations.literal: He submitted memorials; they were not accepted for forwarding.
translations.idiomatic: He filed memorials, but the court refused to transmit them.

sentences.zh: 謀篡死罪囚。

translations.literal: He plotted to seize condemned prisoners facing death.
translations.idiomatic: He conspired to spring men condemned to death from prison.

sentences.zh: 有司請誅，上不忍，削立五縣。

translations.literal: The responsible officials requested execution; the sovereign could not bear it; five counties were stripped from Li.
translations.idiomatic: Prosecutors again demanded his head; the emperor spared him but carved five more counties from his fief.

sentences.zh: 哀帝建平中，立復殺人。

translations.literal: In Emperor Ai’s Jianping period, Li again killed a person.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ai’s Jianping reign he killed yet another man.

sentences.zh: 天子遣廷尉賞、大鴻鼐由持節即訊。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven sent Commandant of Justice Shang and Grand Herald You, bearing credentials, to go and interrogate on the spot.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent Commandant of Justice Shang and Grand Herald You, rod of authority in hand, to take his deposition on site in Liang.

sentences.zh: 至，移書傅、相、中尉曰：「王背策戒，悖暴妄行，連犯大辟，毒流吏民。

translations.literal: When they arrived, they sent documents to tutor, chancellor, and commandant of the capital guard, saying: “The king has turned his back on the admonitions in his investiture patent; he acts perversely and violently, repeatedly violating capital crimes; poison flows to clerks and commoners.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival they wrote to his tutor, chancellor, and capital commandant: “The king has spurned the warnings sealed in his patent of enfeoffment; he behaves like a tyrant, piles up capital offenses, and spreads misery through officials and people alike.”

sentences.zh: 比比蒙恩，不伏重誅，不思改過，復賊殺人。

translations.literal: Time after time he has received grace, not submitting to heavy execution, not thinking to reform his faults; again he has murdered people treacherously.
translations.idiomatic: Again and again he has been pardoned the death he deserved, yet he refuses to mend his ways and has just murdered again.

sentences.zh: 幸得蒙恩，丞相長史、大鴻臚丞即問。

translations.literal: Fortunately having received grace, the chief clerk of the chancellor and the assistant to the grand herald shall go and question.
translations.idiomatic: Since he has already been spared once, the chief clerk of the chancellor and an assistant grand herald are hereby ordered to question him without delay.”

sentences.zh: 王陽病抵讕，置辭驕嫚，不首主令，與背畔亡異。

translations.literal: The king feigns illness and stalls with evasions; his submitted statements are arrogant and rude; he will not confess to the orders of his superiors—no different from rebellion and flight.
translations.idiomatic: He pretends illness, answers with contempt, and refuses to acknowledge his superiors’ authority—as good as open revolt.

sentences.zh: 丞相、御史請收王璽綬，送陳留獄。

translations.literal: The chancellor and imperial clerk request to seize the king’s seal and ribbon and send him to prison in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor and censorate now ask that his seal and sash be seized and that he be remanded to the jail at Chenliu.

sentences.zh: 明詔加恩，復遣廷尉、大鴻臚雜問。

translations.literal: The enlightened edict added grace; again they sent the commandant of justice and grand herald jointly to question.
translations.idiomatic: An edict tempered justice with mercy and ordered the commandant of justice and grand herald to conduct a joint hearing.

sentences.zh: 今王當受詔置辭，恐復不首實對。

translations.literal: Now the king ought to receive the edict and set forth his plea; I fear again he will not confess truth in reply.
translations.idiomatic: The king must respond under that edict, yet he will probably still refuse a truthful account.”

sentences.zh: 《書》曰：『至於再三，有不用，我降爾命。』

translations.literal: The Documents says: ‘If it comes to the third and fourth time and still there is no compliance, I will lower my command upon you.’
translations.idiomatic: The Documents warn, ‘After repeated warnings, if you still disobey, I will bring my sentence down upon you.’”

sentences.zh: 傅、相、中尉皆以輔正為職，『虎兕出於匣，龜玉毀於匱中，是誰之過也？』

translations.literal: Tutor, chancellor, and commandant of the capital guard all take assisting rectification as their duty—‘When the tiger and rhinoceros escape from their cage, when the tortoise and jade are destroyed in their casket—whose fault is it?’
translations.idiomatic: His tutor, chancellor, and capital commandant exist to set him right—‘If tigers break from their pens or sacred relics shatter in the box, who bears the blame?’”

sentences.zh: 書到，明以誼曉王。

translations.literal: When the document arrives, clearly with righteousness enlighten the king.
translations.idiomatic: When this reaches you, explain the moral stakes to the king in plain terms.”

sentences.zh: 敢復懷詐，罪過益深。

translations.literal: If he dares again harbor deceit, his guilt and fault will deepen.
translations.idiomatic: Further deceit will only deepen his guilt.”

sentences.zh: 傅、相以下，不能輔導，有正法。」

translations.literal: From tutor and chancellor downward, those who cannot assist and guide have regular punishment.”
translations.idiomatic: And any tutor or minister who fails to guide him will face the law as well.”

sentences.zh: 立惶恐，免冠對曰：「立少失父母，孤弱處深宮中，獨與宦者婢妾居，漸漬小國之俗，加以質性下愚，有不可移之姿。

translations.literal: Li in fear took off his cap and replied: “Li in youth lost father and mother; orphaned and weak he dwelt in the deep palace; alone with eunuchs, maids, and concubines he lived; gradually he was steeped in the customs of a small state; added to this his substance and nature are low and stupid; there is an posture that cannot be shifted.
translations.idiomatic: Trembling, Li doffed his cap and said, “I was orphaned young and grew up shut inside the palace with only eunuchs and women; a petty frontier kingdom was all I knew, and I am dull by nature—habits that are not easily broken.

sentences.zh: 往者傅、相亦不純以仁誼輔翼立，大臣皆尚苛刻，刺求微密。

translations.literal: In the past tutor and chancellor also did not purely use benevolence and righteousness to assist and wing Li; great ministers all esteemed harshness, pricking and seeking the minute and secret.
translations.idiomatic: Nor did my former tutors and chancellors guide me with consistent kindness; the high ministers preferred harshness, picking at every trifle.

sentences.zh: 讒臣在其間，左右弄口，積使上下不和，更相眄伺。

translations.literal: Slanderous ministers were among them; those at left and right played with mouths; accumulating, they caused superiors and inferiors not to be harmonious, mutually spying on one another.
translations.idiomatic: Sycophants came between us; attendants whispered poison until court and household eyed each other with suspicion.

sentences.zh: 宮殿之裡，毛□過失，亡不暴陳。

translations.literal: Inside the palace halls, hair-fine faults and errors, none were not violently exposed.
translations.idiomatic: Every trivial fault committed within the palace was trumpeted abroad.

sentences.zh: 當伏重誅，以視海內，數蒙聖恩，得見貰赦。

translations.literal: I ought to sit guilty of heavy execution to display to within the seas; repeatedly I have received sagely grace and been permitted to see pardon and forgiveness.
translations.idiomatic: I deserve execution for the world to see, yet time after time I have been spared by imperial grace.

sentences.zh: 今立自知賊殺中郎曹將，冬月迫促，貪生畏死，即詐僵仆陽病，僥倖得逾於須臾。

translations.literal: Now Li himself knows he murdered Palace Gentleman Cao Jiang; winter months pressed urgently; greedy for life and fearing death, then he feigned stiffness and fell, feigning illness, hoping by luck to get past a moment.
translations.idiomatic: I know I murdered Palace Gentleman Cao Jiang; in the depth of winter, clinging to life, I feigned a seizure, hoping to buy a few moments’ reprieve.

sentences.zh: 謹以實對，伏須重誅。」

translations.literal: Respectfully with truth I reply; I bow and await heavy execution.”
translations.idiomatic: This is the truth as I lay it before you; I await whatever sentence you impose.”

sentences.zh: 時冬月盡，其春大赦，不治。

translations.literal: At the time winter month was exhausted; that spring there was a great amnesty; it was not prosecuted.
translations.idiomatic: Winter ended before a verdict came; a general amnesty in the following spring let the matter die.

sentences.zh: 元始中，立坐與平帝外家中山衛氏交通，新都侯王莽奏廢立為庶人，徙漢中。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshi period, Li sat guilty of communicating with the Zhongshan Wei clan, the empress of Emperor Ping’s maternal kin; Marquis of Xindu Wang Mang memorialized to abolish Li as commoner and move him to Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yuanshi, Li was convicted of dealing with the Wei family of Zhongshan, in-laws of Emperor Ping; Wang Mang of Xindu had him reduced to commoner rank and banished to Hanzhong.

sentences.zh: 立自殺。

translations.literal: Li killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Li took his own life.

sentences.zh: 二十七年，國除。

translations.literal: Twenty-seven years, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-seven years after his accession the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 後二歲，莽白太皇太后立孝王玄孫之曾孫沛郡卒史音為梁王，奉孝王后。

translations.literal: After two years, Mang informed the Grand Empress Dowager and established Yin, a zushi clerk from Pei commandery, as King of Liang—he being the great-grandson (曾孫) of a fourth-generation descendant (玄孫) of King Xiao—to maintain the sacrifices for King Xiao’s queen.
translations.idiomatic: Two years later Wang Mang persuaded the Grand Empress Dowager to enfeoff Yin, a registrar from Pei who stood many generations down from King Xiao in the main line, as king of Liang so that the queen consort of King Xiao would still receive offerings.

sentences.zh: 莽篡，國絕。

translations.literal: When Mang usurped, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized the throne the line was extinguished again.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：梁孝王雖以愛親故王膏腴之地，然會漢家隆盛，百姓殷富，故能殖其貨財，廣其宮室車服。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Although Filial King Wu of Liang because of a dear relation was king over fat land, yet he met the Han house at its height and the hundred surnames abundant and wealthy; therefore he was able to increase his goods and wealth, widen his palaces, chariots, and robes.
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s verdict: King Xiao Wu of Liang owed his rich domain to imperial favor, but he also lived when the Han was at its zenith and the people were prosperous—conditions that let him pile up treasure and expand palaces, chariots, and wardrobe beyond any prince’s needs.

sentences.zh: 然亦僭矣。

translations.literal: Yet also he overstepped.
translations.idiomatic: Even so, he exceeded what a vassal may claim.

sentences.zh: 怙親亡厭，牛禍告罰，卒用憂死，悲夫！

translations.literal: Relying on kinship without satiety, an ox’s portent announced punishment; in the end he used worry and died—how sad!
translations.idiomatic: He trusted blood ties too greedily; heaven sent the monstrous ox as warning, and worry consumed him in the end—a bitter lesson.
